revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:東鳴子温泉]]
{{infobox Onsen | Name = Higashi Naruko Onsen (東鳴子温泉) | image = | Comment = | Location = {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|qid=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|Ōsaki, Miyagi|en|Ōsaki, Miyagi|qid=Q385892|lt=Osaki City|1=Ōsaki, Miyagi}} | Access = Rail: {{ill|Rikuu East Line|en|Rikuu East Line|qid=Q872935|lt=Rikuwa Higashi Line|1=Rikuu East Line}} – get off at {{ill|Naruko-Gotenyu Station|en|Naruko-Gotenyu Station|qid=Q1092587|lt=Naruko Goten-yu Station|1=Naruko-Gotenyu Station}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Brine spring|en|Brine spring|qid=Q11428612|lt=Brine spring|1=Brine spring}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Hot Spring|1=Bicarbonate springs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11567142}}, {{ill|Sulfate Hot Spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate Hot Spring|1=Sulfate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11587674}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 14 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | External Links = http://www.hds-net.co.jp/higasinaruko/ | Special Notes = }}
'''Higashi Naruko Onsen''' (ひがしなるこおんせん) is a {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|qid=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}} located in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|qid=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}{{ill|Ōsaki, Miyagi|en|Ōsaki, Miyagi|qid=Q385892|lt=Osaki City|1=Ōsaki, Miyagi}} (formerly part of {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|qid=Q907389|lt=Mutsu Province|1=Mutsu Province}}, and from the Meiji period onward, part of {{ill|Rikuzen Province|en|Rikuzen Province|qid=Q1192236|lt=Rikuzen Province|1=Rikuzen Province}}) within the {{ill|Naruko Onsen Resort|ja|鳴子温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naruko Onsen Resort|1=Naruko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11675322}}. It is situated along the banks of the {{ill|Arao River|ja|江合川|lt=Arao River|lt_ja=荒雄川（江合川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arao River|1=Eai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11550788}}<ref name="env">[https://www.env.go.jp/nature/onsen/area/naruko.pdf 鳴子温泉郷国民保養温泉地計画書] {{ill|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|en|Ministry of the Environment (Japan)|qid=Q1125558|lt=Ministry of the Environment|1=Ministry of the Environment (Japan)}}, accessed September 27, 2022.</ref>.
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Brine spring|en|Brine spring|qid=Q11428612|lt=Brine spring|1=Brine spring}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Sulfate spring|ja|硫酸塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfate spring|qq=}}
Although the quality of the hot spring water varies by source, many of the springs share a common characteristic: they exhibit the properties of a {{ill|Moor hot springs|ja|モール泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor hot springs|qq=}} containing plant degradation components. A mineral odor known as the “moor smell” is present, and in some springs this is compounded by an “oil smell” similar to heavy oil or thinner.
=== Geology === The rock layers throughout the area consist of the Naruko lacustrine strata, volcanic ejecta from the {{ill|Naruko Volcano Group|ja|鳴子火山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naruko Volcano Group|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and accumulations of terrace deposits. In the southwestern direction extending from {{ill|Nakano Onsen|ja|中野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135506075|1=Nakano Onsen}} to {{ill|Walnut Peak|ja|胡桃ヶ岳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Walnut Peak|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, sandstones ({{ill|Sandstone|en|Sandstone|qid=Q13085|lt=Sandstone|1=Sandstone}}) and siltstones (displayed as {{ill|Siltstone|ja|シルト岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siltstone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 泥岩|1=Mudstone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6642424}}) of the Naruko lacustrine strata are chaotically redeposited adjacent to the Naruko Volcano Group, with volcanic gravels ({{ill|Lapilli|en|Lapilli|qid=Q495423|lt=Volcanic Gravel|1=Lapilli}}) incorporated within the Naruko strata—features that attest to the structural development accompanying the formation of the Naruko Volcano. Alluvial sands and gravel layers ({{ill|Alluvium|en|Alluvium|qid=Q6185405|lt=Alluvium|1=Alluvium}}) are widely distributed across the region, under which the Naruko lacustrine strata lie; here, conglomerates and tuffaceous sandstones ({{ill|Tuffaceous Sandstone|ja|凝灰岩質砂岩|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tuffaceous Sandstone|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) have been repeatedly deposited.<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|和書|title=宮城県鳴子町温泉源基盤整備調査報告書 : 東鳴子・赤湯・車湯地区|date=1975|publisher=宮城県衛生部|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/9669325/1/1}}</ref>
=== Sources === There are three theories regarding the mechanism of hot spring emergence: one posits that the water rises along fault lines, another suggests it moves like groundwater within an aquifer—akin to a river or lake—and a third intermediate model combines aspects of both. The spring known as '''Akayu''' is considered to be of a combined type, where energy supplied from the underlying fault transforms the groundwater within the Naruko lacustrine strata into hot spring water, and in which high-temperature volcanic water from the Naruko Volcano mixes in. Many of the hot springs supplied along fault lines are said to be of this type.<ref name=":0" />
Both '''Tanaka Onsen''' and '''Akayu''' frequently yield hot spring water that is rich in {{ill|Bicarbonate|en|Bicarbonate|qid=Q409202|lt=Bicarbonate|1=Bicarbonate}}—known as a soda-type ({{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Hot Spring|1=Bicarbonate springs|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11567142}}) water, typifying an HCO<sub>3</sub><sup>-</sup> system. Water of this type is relatively low in temperature and has a low chloride (Cl<sup>-</sup>) content.
On the northern bank of the {{ill|Arao River|ja|江合川|lt=Arao River|lt_ja=荒雄川（江合川）|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arao River|1=Eai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11550788}}, concentrations of {{ill|Calcium|en|Calcium|qid=Q706|lt=calcium|1=Calcium}} and {{ill|Magnesium|en|Magnesium|qid=Q660|lt=magnesium|1=Magnesium}} are high—likely leached from calcium- and magnesium-rich rock layers under certain conditions.
== Hot Spring Town == The hot spring town is located in a quiet area along the banks of the Arao River, approximately 1.5 km from the center of the Naruko Onsen Resort. Until its merger with {{ill|Onikuboshi Village|ja|鬼首村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onikuboshi Village|1=Onikobe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11673568}} and {{ill|Naruko, Miyagi|en|Naruko, Miyagi|qid=Q6966442|lt=Naruko Town|1=Naruko, Miyagi}} in Shōwa 29, it belonged to {{ill|Kawawata Village|ja|川渡村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawawata Village|1=Kawatabi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11478931}}.
The neighboring hot spring areas, collectively known as the "Tamatsukuri Hachiyu" (Eight Hot Springs), include: * {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子（湯元）、河原湯、元車湯、新車湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naruko Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Nakayamahei Onsen|ja|中山平温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayamahei Onsen|1=Nakayama Daira Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11364155}} (abbreviated as “Nakayama”) * '''Akayu''' and '''Tanaka''' * {{ill|Kawawata Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawawata Onsen|1=Kawatabi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11478932}}
Of these, the two springs, ''Akayu'' and ''Tanaka'', are now considered to constitute the present-day '''Higashi Naruko Onsen'''.
There are 14 {{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|qid=Q562930|lt=ryokan|1=Ryokan}} in the area. The hot spring town is broadly divided into three sections: the area surrounding the station, the area on the opposite bank of the {{ill|Arao River|ja|江合川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arao River|1=Eai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11550788}}, and the area extending southeast from the station along the old road toward {{ill|Japan National Route 47|en|Japan National Route 47|qid=Q6157632|lt=National Route 47|1=Japan National Route 47}}. The hot springs on the southeast side of the station each bear the name of their respective local area—for example, {{ill|Baba Onsen|ja|馬場温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baba Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tanaka Onsen|ja|田中温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanaka Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Nakano Onsen|ja|中野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135506075|1=Nakano Onsen}}.
Because the area has long functioned as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing|ja|湯治場|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=Thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1004887}} site, many ryokan offer lodging without meals or with self-catering facilities, and often feature mixed-gender baths.
=== Therapeutic Bathing Facilities === A distinctive characteristic of the Naruko Onsen Resort is its longstanding role as a place of rest and recuperation—a “bone rest” and “therapeutic venue”—for local workers in the primary industries, such as farmers and fishermen.
During the Edo period, most therapeutic bathers were farmers from the Sendai domain, typically visiting during the off-season. Many were repeat visitors, and each spring had its own regular clientele based on their bathing purposes. In addition to farmers, people from various social classes also came for therapeutic bathing. Bathing sessions were organized in cycles of seven days, which marked the standard duration of a stay; typically, visitors stayed for two or three cycles. Guests usually prepared their own meals, with daily necessities either brought from home or purchased at or near the inns. Some inns even offered recreational facilities such as games of shogi or go. Although the baths were mixed-gender throughout, special bathing areas were provided for high-status individuals.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Keiichi Sasaki|year=1973-10|title=Symposium “On the Hot Springs of Miyagi Prefecture” : Part 1 – The History of Hot Springs in Miyagi Prefecture|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/8422360/1/1|journal=Onsen Kagaku. 24(2)|pages=245–256}}</ref>
=== Communal Baths === A communal public bath exclusively for local residents, known as the “Akayu Communal Bath,” is situated in the central part of the hot spring town.
== History == It is said that Higashi Naruko Onsen was born concurrently with Naruko Onsen in the explosion at Toriyagamorin in 837 AD (Shōwa 4)<ref name="env" />. The various springs were known respectively as “Akabai no Yu (Akayu),” “Tanaka no Yu,” and “Washino Yu”; among these, the “Akabai no Yu” had a palace bath (御殿湯) established by the Sendai Domain during the Edo period<ref name="env" />. The name of the current nearest station is derived from this history. Incidentally, the Sendai Domain’s palace bath was also established at {{ill|Aone Onsen|ja|青根温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aone Onsen|1=Aone Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11661969}}.
=== Ancient Period === * In April 837 AD (Shōwa 4), the ''Shoku Nihon Kōki'' records a report from the “Onsen Ishigami” regarding the emergence of hot spring water.
=== Medieval Period === In the 13th century, the work ''Yakumo Gisho'' authored by 【{{ill|Emperor Juntoku|ja|順徳上皇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Juntoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 順徳天皇|1=Emperor Juntoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q357249}}】 lists three famous hot springs of Mutsu—{{ill|Natori-yu|ja|秋保温泉|lt=Natori-yu|lt_ja=名取湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Natori-yu|1=Akiu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11594813}}, {{ill|Iizaka Onsen|en|Iizaka Onsen|qid=Q17192714|lt=Sabahoko-yu|1=Iizaka Onsen}}, and '''Tamatsukuri-yu''' (玉造湯).
=== Early Modern Period === The springs became known as '''Akayu''' and '''Tanaka-yu'''. The ''Akayu'' (literally “Red Hot Spring”) was located centrally between {{ill|Kawawata Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawawata Onsen|1=Kawatabi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11478932}} and {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naruko Onsen|1=Naruko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11675321}}, with a shortcut via {{ill|Kajiyazawa Station|ja|鍛冶谷沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajiyazawa Station|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} providing convenient access. Although the precise date of discovery is unknown, it is said that a farmer named Washizu Takanetou discovered the hot spring and sold it to a local named Rokunojō. Shortly thereafter, a landslide buried the spring; however, after several years it reemerged at its original location, and from that time it came to be called ''Akaume no Yu'' (赤梅の湯). During the Tenmei famine, Rokunojō disappeared; subsequently, three men—Mangorō, Kanchī, and Heiroku—divided his fields into three shares and established a ryokan.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|和書|title=宮城県鉱泉誌|date=明24.7|publisher=永沢小兵衛|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/763493/1/1}}</ref>
''Tanaka-yu'' (田中湯), located between Akayu and Kawawata, is so named because the basin is surrounded entirely by fields. The date of its discovery is unknown. It is also said that the ancestor of the hot spring proprietor, Takahashi Shinsuke (泉主), was once a retainer of the {{ill|Taira clan|ja|伊勢平氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira clan|qq=}}.<ref name=":1" />
* On May 14, 1689 (Genroku 2), the poet {{ill|Matsuo Bashō|en|Matsuo Bashō|qid=Q5676|lt=Matsuo Bashō|1=Matsuo Bashō}} and his disciple {{ill|Kawai Sora|ja|河合曾良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawai Sora|1=Kawai Sora|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11141924}} passed through while en route from {{ill|Historic Monuments and Sites of Hiraizumi|en|Historic Monuments and Sites of Hiraizumi|qid=Q2896261|lt=Hiraizumi|1=Historic Monuments and Sites of Hiraizumi}} via {{ill|Iwadeyama|ja|岩出山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwadeyama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岩出山町|1=Iwadeyama, Miyagi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11474209}} to the {{ill|Three Mountains of Dewa|en|Three Mountains of Dewa|qid=Q1155726|lt=Dewa Sanzan|1=Three Mountains of Dewa}}. * In 1831 (the first year of Tenpō), ''Tanaka-yu'' began to be offered for bathing not only to local villagers but also to patients. * In 1855 (the first year of Ansei), the wife of the head of the Sendai Domain, {{ill|Date Yoshikuni|en|Date Yoshikuni|qid=Q846375|lt=Date Yoshi-kuni|1=Date Yoshikuni}}, stayed at ''Akayu''.<ref name=":1" />
=== Modern and Contemporary === * In 1910 (Meiji 43), a great flood devastated both Akayu and Tanaka-yu, leading to the complete destruction of the hot spring town; however, both were subsequently rebuilt. * On January 25, 1952 (Shōwa 27), the {{ill|Japanese National Railways|en|Japanese National Railways|qid=Q384011|lt=Japanese National Railways|1=Japanese National Railways}} station, '''Higashi Naruko Station''' (ひがしなるごえき), was opened as a petition station due to strong local demand. * On September 20, 2004 (Heisei 16), a new station building was constructed at '''Naruko Goten-yu Station'''. * In 2011 (Heisei 23), the hot spring area served as a secondary evacuation site for victims from the coastal areas of Miyagi Prefecture affected by the Great East Japan Earthquake. * On May 20, 2016 (Heisei 28), the area was designated as a National Public Resort Hot Spring under the name "{{ill|Naruko Onsen Resort|ja|鳴子温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naruko Onsen Resort|1=Naruko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11675322}}".
== Access == * Rail: Take the {{ill|Rikuu East Line|en|Rikuu East Line|qid=Q872935|lt=Rikuwa Higashi Line|1=Rikuu East Line}} and get off at {{ill|Naruko-Gotenyu Station|en|Naruko-Gotenyu Station|qid=Q1092587|lt=Naruko Goten-yu Station|1=Naruko-Gotenyu Station}}. * Highway Bus: From in front of {{ill|Sendai Station (Miyagi)|en|Sendai Station (Miyagi)|qid=Q801429|lt=Sendai Station|1=Sendai Station (Miyagi)}}, take the {{ill|Miyako Bus|ja|ミヤコーバス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyako Bus|1=Miyakoh Bus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11342540}} or the Sendai–Naruko bus line and alight at either {{ill|Nakano Onsen|ja|中野温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakano Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135506075|1=Nakano Onsen}} or {{ill|Higashi Naruko Akayu|ja|東鳴子赤湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Naruko Akayu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Footnotes == === References === {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Naruko Onsen Resort|ja|鳴子温泉郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naruko Onsen Resort|1=Naruko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11675322}} ** {{ill|Naruko Onsen|ja|鳴子温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naruko Onsen|1=Naruko Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11675321}} ** {{ill|Kawawata Onsen|ja|川渡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawawata Onsen|1=Kawatabi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11478932}} ** {{ill|Nakayamahei Onsen|ja|中山平温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayamahei Onsen|1=Nakayama Daira Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11364155}} ** {{ill|Onikuboshi Onsen|ja|鬼首温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onikuboshi Onsen|1=Onikōbe Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11673570}}
== External Links == * [http://www.naruko.gr.jp/] * [http://higasinaruko.michikusa.jp/]
